text.title: Flexibility and Efficiency Implementation Patterns
text.summary: Flexibility mechanisms for novice and expert users — keyboard shortcuts, command palette, progressive disclosure, customization, and batch operations.

section.title: User Spectrum
section.p: The interface must serve: novice (occasional users, need guided paths), intermediate (regular users, learning accelerators), power users (daily use, need shortcuts and automation). Design layers for all three simultaneously.

rule.statement: Implement a command palette (Ctrl+K / Cmd+K) as the single discoverability surface for all shortcuts.
rule.rationale: A command palette serves novice (can search for actions) and expert (can type known shortcut) users from the same UI surface; it is the most efficient single investment in flexibility.

section.title: Keyboard Shortcuts

rule.statement: Show keyboard shortcuts in menu items and tooltips; shortcuts that are not discoverable do not exist for most users.
rule.rationale: Undiscoverable shortcuts are used only by users who read documentation; visible shortcuts in context menus teach users passively during normal use.

rule.statement: Never override Ctrl+W, Ctrl+N, Ctrl+T, or Ctrl+F in a web application.
rule.rationale: These browser-native shortcuts (close tab, new window, new tab, find) are deeply ingrained; overriding them creates severe friction and breaks platform expectations.

section.p: Layered accessibility: Level 1 = menu item (visible to all); Level 2 = keyboard shortcut shown in menu; Level 3 = expert knows by heart. All three levels from the same menu item.

example.note: Figma: Cmd+/ opens quick actions (command palette), showing all shortcuts with labels. New users discover shortcuts passively; experts use them daily.
Complex dashboard with no keyboard shortcuts, requiring mouse navigation for every action. Power users forced into slow click-only workflows daily.

rule.statement: Expose the 3-5 most-used settings immediately; place the remaining 80% behind an "Advanced" toggle.
rule.rationale: Showing all settings at once overwhelms novice users; hiding primary settings frustrates experts. The 80/20 split satisfies both.

rule.statement: For each action that affects a single item, design a batch variant as an accelerator layer.
rule.rationale: Single-item actions work for novices; batch operations unlock expert-level efficiency for high-volume workflows without adding complexity to the basic flow.

text.reference: Extract keyboard event listeners from JS/TS codebase
